summary: This second part of Simon Späti's data engineering toolkit series covers advanced infrastructure concerns: data processing tools, DevOps practices, and the expanding role of infrastructure as code. The post argues that modern data engineers must master not just SQL and Python, but also Kubernetes, Terraform, and GitOps practices to build production-grade platforms. Späti presents a comprehensive catalog of tools across databases, orchestration, BI, containerization, and observability, while noting that AI-enhanced workflows are reshaping pipeline development. The piece concludes that soft skills and business understanding are increasingly crucial as AI reduces pure coding demands.
key_points: Data engineers now face infrastructure complexity similar to how data scientists once spent 80% of time on data wrangling—DevOps and IaC have become core competencies
GitOps and declarative configurations transform scattered server management into maintainable, reproducible deployments through YAML-based version-controlled infrastructure
DevOps operates on a spectrum of abstraction levels—from serverless to managed Kubernetes—and teams should match abstraction to expertise rather than defaulting to maximum control
AI-enhanced tools with MCP integrations and agentic workflows are actively reshaping how data engineers build and interact with pipelines
As AI reduces coding demands, business acumen and communication skills become the differentiator that transforms data engineers into 10x contributors
money_quotes: One day, you're optimizing SQL queries, the next, you're debugging Kubernetes deployments, and by afternoon, you'll be explaining data quality metrics to stakeholders who just want to know why their dashboard is empty.
SQL is the language of data. SQL is a fundamental skill for doing any data work. There's almost nothing you do without needing SQL.
DevOps isn't binary; it's about selecting the appropriate level of control and abstraction for your specific needs.
Start with higher abstraction levels for faster delivery, then move toward more control only when specific customizations become necessary.
Setting up GitOps for new data projects is hard and best done by having a central team specializing in deployment and operations with deep knowledge.
While you can be a technical expert without these skills, combining technical expertise with strong business understanding and communication will set you apart.
Technical expertise alone isn't always enough; strong communication skills and business understanding transform data engineers into 10x contributors.
key_insight: Modern data engineering requires mastering a spectrum of DevOps abstraction levels and infrastructure tooling, but as AI handles more coding tasks, soft skills and business understanding become the true differentiators.
